name: PoV Lecture 22: Kya Aapke Sheher Mein Gaon Hai?
description: #lecture22: Kya Aapke Sheher Mein Gaon Hai?What happens to a village when the city slowly grows around it?Delhi often feels like a city that never stops expanding. Flyovers stretch further each year, glass towers climb higher, and neighbourhoods appear where fields once stood. Yet hidden within the city are places that seem to follow a completely different rhythm. Step off the main road and the lanes suddenly narrow. A buffalo might be tied outside a home, and a centuries-old mosque appears quietly between tightly packed buildings.These are Delhi’s urban villages.For this lecture, Dr. Ekta Chauhan takes us into these neighbourhoods through stories, humour, and lived experience. Born and raised in Khirki village, she brings a rare insider’s perspective to places where ancestral settlements and rapid urban development collide every day.Drawing from her book Sheher Mein Gaon and years of research, the talk explores how migrants, heritage monuments, cafés, speculative real estate, and old village homes all end up sharing the same streets. It asks a deceptively simple question: when the city arrives, what becomes of the village? Does it disappear, adapt to urban life, or slowly transform into something entirely unexpected?Join us for an evening that invites us to look at Delhi a little differently, through the lanes, histories, and everyday negotiations of its urban villages.About the SpeakerDr. Ekta Chauhan is a researcher of cities, cultures, and communities and an Assistant Professor at the Jindal School of Design and Architecture, O.P. Jindal Global University. She holds a B.A. (Hons.) in History from St. Stephen’s College, a Master’s degree in Public Policy from the National Law School of India University, Bangalore, and a PhD in World Heritage Studies from BTU Cottbus, Germany.Born and raised in Khirki village in Delhi, her work explores the rural–urban dynamics of the city and the everyday lives of communities negotiating rapid urban change. Her recent book Sheher Mein Gaon traces Delhi’s history through the stories and transformations of its urban villages.Her engagement with storytelling began in 2016 when she started leading heritage walks in Khirki village. Over the past decade, she has hosted more than 5,000 visitors including tourists, students, locals, photographers, and researchers, guiding them through heritage sites such as Satpula and Khirki Masjid as well as the village’s lanes, homes, and cultural landmarks.📌 Note: Alcohol will be served only to guests aged 25 and above. Non-alcoholic options will be available. Attendance implies consent to being filmed for event documentation.

location.address: D Block, D12, Market, above Bank Of India, Vasant Vihar, New Delhi, Delhi 110057, India

name: Daawat-e-Iftar in Jama Masjid
description: The month of Ramzan brings with itself unprecedented festive cheer in Purani Dilli. While the euphoria can be experienced all around, the royal Jama Masjid is undoubtedly the heart of it all. Special Ramzan markets operate in full fervour all around the mosque and people gather in its courtyards before sunset to break their fasts. These are traditions that have been observed in this Old City since centuries and till date, the community spirit that can be witnessed in Jama Masjid’s courtyard remains unparalleled. What to expect: This will be more of a gathering and not a walk. Ofcourse I’ll be sharing various stories about the culture and my personal experiences. We will make fruit chat and roohafza together and there will be other food items as well which we generally serve during iftars in my family. As the sun sets, we will experience the aura of the evening prayer and eat together, post which we’ll disperse by 7:30pm.

location.address: Jama Masjid, Old Delhi, Delhi, India

name: Delhi School of Ideas - Gully Labs x unLecture
description: The Delhi School of Ideas by Gully labs x unLecture: How Weird Can Research Get? What happens when research leaves the classroom and lands in a bar? Gully Labs teams up with unLecture, the Delhi community known for bringing big ideas out of lecture halls and into cafés and bars, for the first edition of Delhi School of Ideas, dedicated to strange questions, curious minds, and conversations that refuse to stay academic.This edition asks a simple question: How weird can research get?Expect a series of short, sharp presentations where speakers unpack their most unusual ideas, experiments, and niche obsessions. From unexpected cultural insights to bizarre innovations and wonderfully unnecessary investigations, we've got it all. The catch? You’ll be hearing all of this with unlimited Kati Patang beer and BRB chips on the house.Whether it’s niche anthropology, obscure design experiments, hyper-specific cultural rabbit holes, or research nobody asked for but everyone wants to hear, this is a night for intellectual chaos and delightful curiosity.Come for the drinks. Stay for the ideas. Leave with at least one question you didn’t know you needed answered.14th March | The Baithak, Gully Labs, Panchsheel | Gates open 4: 30pm.

location.address: Local Shopping Center, 14, Panchsheel Park North, Panchsheel Park, New Delhi, Delhi 110017, India

name: Delhi Coffee Crawl
description: Step inside The Hazelnut Factory, Lucknow’s beloved cafe space and home to India’s first espresso bar in Delhi, for an evening that traces coffee from origin to final pour.The Hazelnut Factory was born in 2019 from founder Ankit Sahni’s vision to bring specialty coffee, freshly baked goods, and over 70 varieties of handcrafted sweets under one roof. Today, it welcomes 1,50,000 visitors a month and stands as UP’s fastest growing F&B brand.For one evening, you experience it from the inside out.The Delhi Coffee Crawl by CTRLlab brings together 15-20 guests inside this handpicked cafe for an intimate 1.5-2hr journey.Guided by the people who built the space, you’ll move through story, process, scent, and flavor — sipping and smelling your way through a tasting flight, watching and learning, finding your chosen cup, and closing the evening over conversation and a collectible crawl archive.One space. One evening. One full lifecycle of coffee.Capacity: 10–15 guests · Duration: ~2 hours

location.address: GF-K1, Ground Floor, Worldmark 2, Asset Area 8, Aerocity, New Delhi, Delhi 110037, India

name: Rang-e-Khusrau
description: An ode to Amir Khusrau, a lyrical homage to Amir Khusro — poet, sufi and bon vivant. It is a celebration of love in its many shades, and of the poet who gave it voice with wit, devotion and dazzling invention.Presented by Vidya Shah and Chandrasekhar, the performance journeys through Khusro’s life and verse.At its heart lies prem — love as longing, surrender, wit, and transcendence. In Khusro’s verses, language becomes a meeting ground, dissolving boundaries of faith and form, shaping a shared aesthetic that continues to resonate today.We invite you to join us for an evening that celebrates the enduring dialogue between legend,  poetry and song, and the timeless emotions that continue to bind us across centuries.

location.address: 1237, Gali Khatikan, Imli Mohalla, Mohalla IMLI, Kucha Pati Ram, Sitaram Bazar, Delhi, 110006, India

name: What Gets Funded Gets Published
description: Join us for a lecture in a bar, that asks a question most news consumers rarely pause to consider:Who really pays for the news you read?About the LectureFor most audiences today, journalism functions much like any other product or service, shaped by business models, incentives, and the financial structures that sustain it. Just like education, healthcare, or streaming platforms, news organisations must operate within economic systems that determine what survives and what disappears.So what happens when journalism is funded by corporate advertising? Or when governments become the primary source of financial support? But, there's a way for audiences to intersect - healthily, happily, with utility - with news organisations that aren't run by corporate or governments. This talk will explain how.About the SpeakerAbhinandan Sekhri is the cofounder and CEO of Newslaundry, one of India’s leading independent digital news platforms built on a subscription-driven model.He also cofounded Small Screen, a production house creating television, film, and digital content. Before launching Newslaundry, he worked as a producer, director, and writer on several television projects, including the award-winning food and travel show Highway on My Plate, as well as satirical news programmes such as Gustaakhi Maaf and The Great Indian Tamasha.Earlier in his career, he worked as a reporter with the India Today Group from 1995 to 1999.Through Newslaundry, he has been a vocal advocate for reader-funded journalism and media accountability in India.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

location.address: M9, Level, block market, 2, M, Block M, Greater Kailash II, Greater Kailash, New Delhi, Delhi 110048, India

name: ALT EFF Film Club - Delhi | 17th April
description: If you are tired of the outrage cycle, doomscrolling collapse, and the looming AI takeover narrative. If you have caught yourself going numb just to cope. We see you.All Living Things Environmental Film Festival is back with the third season of Film Club. A monthly meet up across 10 cities over 5 months, orbiting environmental cinema that cracks you open without trapping you in doom and gloom.It is a room full of people choosing to show up in cities whose infrastructure persuades you to stay home, choosing to sit together, watch together, and turn private anxiety into collective imagination. Messy. Human. Honest conversation.With snacks. Always snacks.APRIL CURATION: "The Climate Baby Dilemma"Synopsis:"The Climate Baby Dilemma" by filmmaker Victoria Lean follows science writer Britt Wray as she explores the decision to have children amid climate change. Over three years, she engages with activists and parents, reflecting generational concerns. Featuring Sarain Fox and Severn Suzuki, the film offers insights on transforming climate anxiety into action.PROGRAMME17th April 2026, Friday7:00 - 9:00 PM: The Climate Baby Dilemma | Victoria Lean | Canada | 44 mins | English | 2022All activities, including discussions and refreshments, will occur between 7:00 and 9:00 PM.What's Included?Film screeningGroup discussionLight refreshmentsCommunity spaceStudent Discount50% off for students with a valid ID (& must bring ID to the screening).CODE: ALTSTUDENTABOUT ALT EFFAll Living Things Environmental Film Festival (ALT EFF) is the world’s largest decentralised film festival and a premiere showcase for environmental cinema.Across its annual festival, monthly Film Club, schools initiatives, film fund, and year round curations, ALT EFF nurtures stories grounded in care, collective responsibility, stewardship, and an abiding love for the natural world.Spanning countries, genres, languages, and forms, it brings together stories reimagining our relationship with all living things.

location.address: R 84, opposite Select Citywalk, Block J, Khirki Extension, Malviya Nagar, New Delhi, Delhi 110017, India

organizer.name: All Living Things Environmental Film Festival

name: The unCommon In Common Sense
description: Join us for a lecture that begins with a simple riddle, and ends by unsettling one of our deepest reflexes.About the LectureWhen a plane crashes, we seldom blame aeronautical engineers. When a medicine fails someone we know, we seldom blame doctors as a profession. When social media wreaks havoc, we rarely summon technologists to account for every social consequence.And yet there is one group we blame for almost everything.When markets fail. When prices rise. When growth slows. When inequality deepens. When policy backfires. You turn to them, often with accusation, sometimes with expectation, and demand that they fix what may not even be theirs to fix.Who are they? Blending humour, policy experience, and intellectual provocation, the session invites us to look at what we casually call “common sense” and to discover what is deeply uncommon about it.About the SpeakerShubhashis Gangopadhyay received his PhD in Economics from Cornell University in 1983. He joined the Indian Statistical Institute the same year and became a full professor in 1991. He founded the India Development Foundation in 2003 and currently serves as its Managing Trustee and Research Director.Over the course of his career, he has helped establish major academic institutions, including the School of Humanities and Social Sciences at Shiv Nadar University, the Indian School of Public Policy, and the School of Liberal Studies at UPES. Internationally, he has held the Chair Professorship of Emerging Market Finance at the University of Groningen and continues as Professor at the University of Gothenburg, Sweden.In 2008, during the global financial crisis, he was appointed Advisor to the Finance Minister of India. He has served on advisory councils for the World Bank, the Competition Commission of India, and several global academic institutions. His work has shaped major policy reforms in India, including steel decontrol and energy pricing reforms.A widely published scholar with numerous books and articles in economics and finance, his current research spans policy, finance, and law — informed by decades of engagement at the intersection of academia, government, and industry.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

location.address: Shop No: 229, FIRST FLOOR, DLF Avenue Mall, 232, Mandir Marg, Saket District Centre, District Centre, Sector 6, Saket, New Delhi, Delhi 110017, India

name: Can AI Predict Politics?
description: Join us for a lecture that asks a provocative question: What if geopolitical shocks are not unpredictable, only poorly measured?About the LectureFrom unexpected election outcomes to global ruptures such as the Arab Spring or Brexit, the world repeatedly confronts “surprise” events that destabilise political systems. But are these events truly unforeseeable, or have we simply lacked the right tools to model them?This lecture explores a critical frontier in strategic policy research: the scientific measurement of cognitive index and rationality within complex systems.Moving beyond speculative AI optimism, the session examines how validated cognitive–machine learning models can forecast disruptive geopolitical transitions with increasing precision. Rather than focusing on whether artificial intelligence can predict political events, the real question becomes: which models deliver reliable, empirically grounded results?At its core, the lecture argues that instability is not random. Systems exhibit measurable patterns of rationality and breakdown, and understanding these patterns may reshape how we anticipate the future of governance.About the SpeakerRaúl V. Rodríguez is Vice President at Woxsen University and Sir Cary Cooper Professor of Organizational Behaviour. A computational social scientist, his work models intelligence, rationality, and failure across complex political, organizational, and technological systems.He has authored over 70 peer-reviewed publications and 20 books, with research spanning artificial intelligence, cognitive science, decision theory, necrobotics, and strategic behavior. Dr. Rodríguez serves on multiple international advisory boards, contributes to global policy and governance initiatives, and regularly speaks at major international platforms including World Summit AI, Web Summit, and Davos.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from St. Stephen’s, Delhi who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

location.address: Commercial Complex B 6/7-22 Opp Deer Park, Block B6, Humayunpur, Safdarjung Enclave, New Delhi, Delhi 110029, India

name: Who Gets To Belong In A City?
description: Who is a city actually built for?About the LecturePublic space is often described as neutral, open, accessible, equally available to all.But everyday experience tells a different story.Who sits comfortably in a park and who scans their surroundings. Who lingers and who rushes. Who takes up space and who shrinks. Who feels at home and who feels watched. These patterns are not incidental. They reflect deeper structures of power embedded in design, governance, and investment.This lecture examines how cities are shaped not just by infrastructure, but by assumptions about whose presence is anticipated, and whose is treated as exceptional.Drawing from fieldwork across Indian cities, including on-ground urban transformation projects in Ayodhya and research in Delhi, alongside global scholarship on gender-responsive planning, The aim of this lecture is not only to critique existing models,  but to offer new ways of seeing, imagining, and building cities.About the SpeakerRuchita Bansal is an urban practitioner and researcher working at the intersection of public space, mobility, and gender in Indian cities. Her work explores how infrastructure decisions shape everyday urban experience, particularly for women and marginalised communities.She has been closely involved in large-scale urban transformation initiatives, including façade improvement and public realm interventions in Ayodhya, working directly with planners, contractors, and local authorities to translate design vision into execution under complex, time-bound conditions.Ruchita is also the founder of SheCity India, a platform documenting and analysing how women experience Indian cities. Through research, storytelling, public dialogue, and the Human City Podcast, she challenges the assumption that public space is neutral — arguing instead that cities mirror the values and power structures embedded within their systems.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from St. Stephen’s, Delhi who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

name: The Garden Chronicles: Whispers of the Azim Bagh
description: Looking for soul-soothing things to do in Delhi this March? Sunder Nursery is easily one of the city's most breathtaking spots—a perfect mix of Mughal grandeur and lush botany. Beyond the manicured lawns of this UNESCO World Heritage site lies a 16th-century saga waiting to be told.Join us for a guided heritage walk through the Azim Bagh, where we swap the city noise for tales of Mughal architecture, restored grandeur, and the secrets of the monuments and trees that have stood the test of time. Grab your walking shoes and a camera—you’ll need both! 📸

location.address: Bharat Scouts and Guides Marg, opposite Humayun Tomb Park, Nizamuddin, Hazrat Nizamuddin, National Zoological Park, Sundar Nagar, New Delhi, Delhi 110013, India

name: Eros Of The Wasteland
description: About the LectureIn 2019, a newspaper headline declared: “The Gazipur landfill will be taller than the Taj Mahal.”This comparison unsettles. The monument and the landfill, beauty and discard, are placed in the same frame. What does it mean that our waste now rivals our most cherished symbols?This lecture explores how the modern wasteland has become a defining landscape of our time. Landfills, mines, extraction zones, war zones, these are often treated as voids, as damaged or empty terrain. Yet they are dense with ecological processes, histories of displacement, and layered human memory.Drawing from literature, landscape, and critical theory, the session asks whether the sublime can still be understood as pure or immaculate. Increasingly, the sublime emerges through barrenness, through sites produced by violent capture and industrial excess. Rather than treating these hauntings as contamination, this lecture reads them as sites of responsibility, spaces where meaning emerges through echoes, residues, and what remains.About the SpeakersSonali Prasad is a journalist and novelist from New Delhi. Her reporting has appeared in publications including The Guardian, The Washington Post, Hakai, Quartz, and Esquire Singapore. She has been awarded the Pulitzer Travelling Fellowship, Global TED Fellowship, MIT Knight Science Journalism Fellowship, Logan Science Journalism Fellowship, and a Jan Michalski Writing Residency, among others. Her debut novel, Glass Bottom (Picador India, 2024), was named a literary debut of the year and shortlisted for multiple awards. She has reported from several Olympic and Paralympic Games and served on the International Olympic Committee Press Committee from 2012 to 2021.Sonakshi Srivastava is a Senior Writing Fellow (ELT) at Ashoka University. A graduate of the University of Delhi, her research examines biopolitics, ability and debility, translation, and posthumanism. She serves as Translation Editor for Usawa Literary Review and as an educational arm assistant at Asymptote. Her work has appeared in Asymptote, Scroll, The Locavore, Moveable Type, and other platforms, and has been supported by fellowships including the ASLE Translation Grant and the Diverse Voices Fellowship.About unLectureunLecture brings experts out of classrooms and into cafés, bars and neighbourhood spaces across Delhi. Created by three friends from college who wanted learning to feel warm, social and alive. unLecture turns an otherwise ordinary evening into a conversation you may think about long after it ends.

location.address: Basement, Ground floor and First floor Building no. 12, Panchsheel Community Centre, Shahpur Jat, Panchsheel Park, New Delhi, Delhi 110017, India

name: Safdarjung Tomb Heritage Walk
description: Safdarjung’s Tomb Heritage WalkJoin us for a journey into the fading glow of Mughal splendour at Safdarjung's Tomb — often called the *last flicker of Mughal grandeur* in Delhi.Built in 1754 for Safdarjung, the powerful Nawab of Awadh and Prime Minister of the Mughal court, this monument stands at the twilight of an empire — grand in ambition, yet carrying within it the quiet signs of decline.Its story is as fascinating as its form. From courtly intrigues and shifting loyalties to the architectural choices that reveal a changing political landscape, Safdarjung’s life opens a compelling window into 18th-century Delhi.We will also trace a lesser-known layer of its history — how this tomb was constructed by reusing material from a pre-existing Mughal structure, quite literally building one memory over another.To truly understand Delhi — its rise, its resilience, and its gradual transitions — a visit to Safdarjung’s Tomb is essential.Come, walk with us through the final chapter of Mughal architectural brilliance — where grandeur meets gravity, and history lingers in every arch.

location.address: Airforce Golf Course, Delhi Race Club, New Delhi, Delhi 110003, India

name: Tracing Djnns of Kotla Ferozshah
description: TRACING THE DJINNS of FEROZSHAH KOTLA FORTAs twilight deepens and shadows lengthen across the old walls, we will gather at the main gate of Feroz Shah Kotla. Before us lie crumbling stone portals, hollowed arches, and silent courtyards that once bustled with life in the 14th century under Sultan Feroz Shah Tughlaq.This is no ordinary heritage walk. That evening, we will tread the boundary between history and myth — stepping into the realm of the djinns said to haunt these ruins, to listen, to judge, and sometimes to grant wishes.Led by expert storyteller and myth-hunter, the walk will weave together folklore, historical lore, and spooky tales when moonlight meets masonry. Expect atmospheric narration, dramatic pauses, unexpected twists — and maybe a few ghostly encounters.Not for the faint hearted !!Highlights * A guided evening walk through ruins, hidden chambers, and alleyways* Folklore and legends of djinns haunting the fort* Dramatic storytelling under the evening skies* Occasional “mystery stops” and whispers in the dark* A themed ritual (writing wishes / invoking legends) in the end.

location.address: J6MV+X87, Balmiki Basti, Vikram Nagar, New Delhi, Delhi, 110002, India

name: Nizamuddin Heritage walk on Sufism & Poetry
description: In the Sufi Bylanes of Nizamuddin — A Heritage Walk of Stories, Shrines & SilenceStep into the tender, time-worn lanes of Nizamuddin, where the fragrance of incense mingles with qawwali echoes and centuries of faith, love, and longing still breathe through stone and sky.Join us for a soulful heritage walk through the sacred precincts of Nizamuddin Dargah, as we trace the footsteps of saints, poets, patrons, and wanderers who made this neighbourhood a living archive of Delhi’s Sufi soul.On this journey, we will also pause at:✨ Ghalib’s Mazar — remembering the poet of longing and lament✨ Chausath Khamba — a marble pavilion of quiet grace and symmetry✨ Atagha Khan’s Tomb — a Mughal-era resting place of power and piety✨ Pateh Shah’s Dargah — where devotion meets deep serenity. Along the way, we’ll listen to stories of shrines and seekers, of migration and memory, of music, miracles, and mysticism — stories that still whisper through these alleys at dusk.

location.address: Bharat Scouts and Guides Marg, opposite Humayun Tomb Park, Nizamuddin, Hazrat Nizamuddin, National Zoological Park, Sundar Nagar, New Delhi, Delhi 110013, India

name: Humayun's Tomb & Museum heritage walk
description: Step into the grandeur of Humayun's Tomb with us for an immersive heritage walk that goes beyond stone and symmetry.We will not only explore the monument’s magnificent architecture, its Persian influences, and its place in the evolution of Mughal design — but also rediscover Humayun himself, perhaps the most underestimated of the Mughals. His struggles, exile, return, and legacy shaped the empire in ways we rarely pause to notice.Our journey will then continue to the Humayun's Tomb Museum — because no visit to the tomb is truly complete without experiencing the stories, artefacts, and narratives preserved there.Come, walk through history — and meet the emperor behind the monument.

name: UNCORKED: A Guided Wine Tasting & Pairing
description: This isn’t your usual wine tasting.And it’s not meant to be.This is a guided experience  where you understandwhy brie works with one wine,why goat cheese behaves differently,why chocolate surprises you with another,and why some wines simply need time.You’re guided step by step by a sommelier who lives and breathes wine.You taste. You ask. You talk. You linger.You’ll share the table with people you haven’t met before, and one of kind experienceThis will include 5 tasting wines paired thoughtfully with small bites. Additional wines can be purchased at the event later.This event will take place at our residence in Gurgaon. ( Exact location will be shared 24 hours before the event to maintain privacyUncork with us.

Anyone who wishes to share their art can take the stage for a few minutes.You are welcome to attend alone, with friends, family, or your partner. Sangam is a space for those who value performing arts and meaningful conversations. It offers an opportunity to step away from daily routines, pause, and immerse yourself in a shared community experience.

description: Mahjong Episode 1.2 by VamoroAfter the incredible response to our previous Mahjong sessions, Vamoro is thrilled to bring back another thoughtfully curated Mahjong experience.At Vamoro, we design experiences that add value to everyday living whether through play, learning, or community. Mahjong Episode 1.2 is a guided, interactive session created to help you slow down, sharpen your strategic thinking, connect with new people, and pick up a skill that has travelled across cultures for centuries.Whether you’re a curious beginner or an eager enthusiast, join us for an evening that blends learning, laughter, and a touch of mindful play.

name: Pakhala Pop Up : A Summer Table from Odisha
description: Pakhala Pop-Up: A Summer Table from OdishaA Rosei Ghara x Kartik’s Kitchen CollaborationAs the Delhi sun begins to turn warmer, we bring to the table a ritual from coastal Odisha — a meal designed for summer.Pakhala is not just fermented rice. It is comfort, cooling, seasonal wisdom, and memory served in a bowl.On 21st & 22nd March, from 12 pm onwards, Rosei Ghara, in collaboration with Kartik’s Kitchen, invites you to experience an Odia summer table — thoughtfully curated, slow, and rooted in tradition.What to Expect:At the centre sits Pakhala — lightly fermented rice, cooling and probiotic-rich — accompanied by an abundant spread of classic summer sides. Expect crisp fries, smoky mash, leafy greens, lentil badis, seasonal vegetables, and carefully spiced accompaniments — each dish balancing texture, heat, tang, and crunch.Why It’s Special• A rare opportunity to experience Odisha’s iconic summer meal in Delhi• A collaboration between two kitchens that celebrate regional Indian flavours• Limited seating to preserve the intimacy of the table• Hosted in a private residence in Jangpura Extension (complete address shared post-booking)• A slow, immersive lunch designed around seasonality and traditionPakhala is typically eaten for lunch in Odia homes — and we invite you to experience it exactly as it is meant to be enjoyed: unhurried, generous, and shared.📍 Venue: Jangpura Extension, Delhi🗓️ Dates: 21st & 22nd March🕛 Time: 12 pm onwards✨ Limited seats | Community dining experienceCome curious. Come hungry.Leave with a new understanding of how Odisha does summer.

location.address: Jungpura Extension, Jangpura, New Delhi, Delhi, India

name: #lecture: Drinks, Divides, & the Drama of Borders
description: #lectureWhen was the last time you attended a bar talk by a documentary film maker on the daily realities and complexities of people living in India's border areas?When we think of borders, popular culture often conjures images of conflict. What’s usually missing from these images are the very people who reside in border areas, for whom negotiating the border is not an exotic idea, but a daily reality. The lecture will take you on a kaleidoscopic journey to India’s deepest recesses and explore the everyday complexities of life at India’s margins. Through images, videos, anecdotes, and songs, we will meet common Indians who deal with borders in their own unique ways and come out with light, humor, and hope from the darkest possibilities. Touching the themes of migration, kinship, and fluid identities, this lecture invites you to look beyond the media’s obsession with “Us vs Them” - towards a more complex and intersectional world of “Us and Them”.We're delighted to present Samarth Mahajan, a Mumbai-based filmmaker with a special interest in narratives from India’s border areas. His work includes two national award-winning documentaries - the latest feature ‘Borderlands’ explores lives defined by India's borders, and the film ‘The Unreserved’ captures stories from general compartments of Indian Railways. He is now writing his narrative feature debut, and developing it at the Writers INK Screenwriting Lab 2024, an idea-to-draft lab supported by Ford Foundation and Netflix.You can learn more about him & his films here → Bio | Work | IMDWhat to Expect:✦ 12:00 PM – Doors open. Arrive early to grab your seat; seating is first come, first served.✦ 12:00 – 12:15 PM – Settle in, order your drinks and food, and ease into the space with a few conversations.✦ 12:15 PM – The lecture begins.✦ 01:00 PM – We open the floor for questions and a shared discussion with the speaker.✦ 1:30 PM onwards – Stay back for more food, drinks, and conversations that linger.About Pint of View DelhiInspired by the Lectures on Tap movement in the US, Pint of View creates informal spaces for serious ideas. It brings together philosophy, science, technology, and art in settings where conversations unfold slowly and critical inquiry feels collective rather than instructional.📌 Note: Alcohol will be served only to guests aged 21 and above. Non-alcoholic options will be available. Attendance implies consent to being filmed for event documentation.

location.address: 32nd avenue, Sector 15 Part 2, Sector 15, Gurugram, Haryana 122001, India

name: HE SAID SHE SAID: Comedy Drama
description: DREAMCATCHERS THEATRE CLUB, GURGAONPRESENTSHE SAID SHE SAID A comedy of mannersFilled with laughter and drama with sharp wit as this classic comedy drama take you on a journey through the quirks of relationships, perceptions, and the misunderstandings that arise from everyday conversations.  Synopsis : He Said She Said by Alice Gerstenberg.  He Said She Said, offers a captivating story centered around a carefully orchestrated conflict by a socialite. The audience is left questioning who is truly telling the truth as two conflicting perspectives unfold, showcasing the chaos and confusion that can result from careless words.Join us for an engaging and highly entertaining live theatre experience that weaves together clever dialogues, unexpected twists, and comical moments that will have you reflecting on the ups and downs of human interactions.Cast: Madhu Kandhari, Sangeeta Dixit Chakraborty, Sangeeta Rathi, Nikhil RajeAbout the groupDreamcatchers, the vibrant theater group based in Gurgaon, is a creative powerhouse bringing fresh energy to the stage with our innovative storytelling! Our passionate team consists of enthusiastic theater lovers—actors, singers, musicians, dancers, writers, and artists—dedicated to nurturing the performing arts in our country through their talents and support. Don’t miss out on these incredible stories—stay tuned for a memorable theatrical experience!

name: 4 sessions package TAI CHI WITH RASHID
description: VALID FOR 4 SESSIONS UNTIL END OF MAY 2026 Join the Tai Chi Practice Circle with sensei Rashid Ansari for cultivating wellness, calmness, grounding and inner balance with the transformative practices of Tai Chi and Qi Gong. Held on Sundays at Lilanoor Centre. Hosted by IT'S ALL GOOD.Starting March 15th.Cost : Rs. 4800/- for 4 sessions (Student Rs. 2000/-). IMPORTANT NOTE: To book this 4-SESSION PAGKAGE, please click on the session given: MAY 31st. It means that you may attend ANY 4 Sunday Tai Chi sessions until the end of May, 2026. Thank you. [You may also book a single session as a DROP-IN (Rs. 1500/- per session; Student Rs. 600/-). See specific date in the main listing for booking, or simply drop in and pay on the spot.] ***Both Tai Chi and Qi Gong offer a unique perspective of being a Participant and Observer simultaneously.Tai chi, often called Meditation in Motion, is an incredibly potent system of mind body synergy and a block and tackle system, working on the joints and angles of the body.regulates blood pressure and hypertension.the different movements work on the different organs and glands, helping and energising them to work at optimum potency.strengthens the muscles and tendons, aligns the skeletal and neuromuscular system.helps to deal with anxiety and mental health issues.can be practiced by anybody - young and old alike.Rashid Ansari has been practicing/teaching the martial arts , dance, movement, choreography and holistic health/wellness for over 50 years now, both in India and various parts of the world. In addition to martial arts, Ansari is a contemporary dance instructor and choreographer. He has explored physical theatre and theatre movement, integrating these disciplines into his teaching methodology. He founded and serves as the artistic director of Mobius Strip, a dance theatre company, and Earth Rhythms, a world music ensemble. He also conducts classes and workshops in “Dance your Senses - The 5 Rhythms.” A organic movement methodology based on the individual’s own intrinsic sense of movement and emotions - as a means of finding one’s own movement and dealing with physical, emotional and intellectual blocks.***IT'S ALL GOOD | The Science and Art of JoyMany people feel stuck, exasperated, low, or just plain bored in their lives. name: Curated Tour of Lodhi Art District by St+art India
description: Lodhi Art DistrictLodhi Art District is India’s first ongoing art district. An open-air museum by St+art India Foundation, the pedestrian-friendly colony houses over 65+ murals by renowned local and international artists. The area serves as evidence of the possibility of reimagining public spaces, bringing us back in touch with our city, its history, and the many narratives that illuminate it. Sign up for an immersive walk guided by a St+art team member to discover the murals, consider how public spaces can be utilised, and become a part of this cultural transformation.Meeting Point: Indian Habitat Centre, Gate 3About the TourThe curated tours are a walkthrough of the vibrant public art district. They offer an interactive exploration through which St+art introduces new ways of experiencing contemporary art and the cityscape. These tours provide a complete experience, delving into the historical aspects of the location, the stories behind the project, and the murals themselves. Participants will actively witness the enhancement of the city and the reimagining of its public spaces. A tour leader from the St+art team will provide insights into the background and context of the artworks, the area, and its cultural significance in the city. (Nearby attractions include Safdarjung Tomb, Lodhi Garden, and Khan Market). About St+artSt+art India Foundation contributes to urban regeneration and community living through contemporary urban art projects. The foundation enables a vision for democratised public spaces through interdisciplinary art interventions that are rooted in the social context.Since 2014, with the support of Asian paints, the foundation has organised multiple festivals and public art projects in Delhi, Mumbai, Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Kolkata, Chandigarh and Coimbatore among others. Each public art district in the country and editions of festivals bring curated interventions to civic spaces that are embedded in urban culture, and use art as a tool to reimagine how public spaces can be utilised. For the foundation, which has been responsible for making urban art a movement in India, public art interventions are a celebration of the street as a canvas for visual creativity.

location.address: India Habitat Centre Gate 3, Vardhman Marg, Block 20, Institutional Area, Lodi Colony, New Delhi, Delhi 110003, India

name: Dinner with strangers by StepOut (South Delhi)
description: Serendipity on a TableStepOut Dinners bring strangers together at some of the city’s best restaurants for an evening that feels easy, warm, and unexpectedly meaningful.You are seated at a small, thoughtfully curated table with people you would likely never meet otherwise. Different backgrounds, different stories, one shared meal. The crowd is balanced, open minded, and there by choice, not chance. No loud networking. No awkward icebreakers. Just good food, unforced conversations, and the kind of listening that rarely happens in daily life.The restaurants are carefully chosen. Comfortable, well loved spaces where you can settle in and talk without rushing. The focus is not on the menu alone, but on the atmosphere that allows connection to unfold naturally.Some nights are light and playful. Some go deeper than expected. Some surprise you quietly. That is the point.StepOut Dinners are not about dating or outcomes. They are about discovery. Showing up. Sitting down. Letting the table do its quiet work.Sometimes, all it takes is one seat at the right table for life to feel a little more open. For more information, visit www.stepout.world

location.address: South Delhi, New Delhi, Delhi, India
